title: Chapter 30: The Fever
content: I don't remember day three.

I remember day one — the sketchpad, the chalk, the hum settling into my knuckles like a promise. I remember day two — the first bolt of fabric unspooling across the cutting table like a flag of surrender. But day three is gone, swallowed by the fever, and I don't miss it. Whatever I made on day three, my hands remember even if my mind doesn't.

The collection has a name now. TIMEPIECE. It came to me somewhere in the blur between midnight and dawn, between the second and third cup of coffee I didn't finish, and once I had the name, everything else locked into place like a mechanism clicking home. Every garment will be a century. Every seam will be a memory. Every fitting will be a confession.

I haven't slept properly in — I don't know how long. The studio clock is buried under pattern pieces and I don't care enough to excavate it. Chalk dust hangs in the air like fog, coats my tongue when I breathe, settles on the windowsills in pale drifts. The radiators click and groan but I don't feel the cold. The hum keeps me warm. The hum keeps me moving. It vibrates through my fingers, into the shears, into the fabric, and every cut feels like an exorcism and a prayer in the same breath.

My phone buzzes. I ignore it. It buzzes again. I ignore it again. On the third buzz I glance down — Suki, all caps: EAT SOMETHING. Below that: I WILL DRAG YOU OUT OF THAT STUDIO. Below that: Ubereats arriving in 10. Don't make me call Sable.

I eat standing up, one hand holding a cold dumpling, the other pinning the collar of the first piece. I don't taste the food. I taste the metal of the pins between my lips, bright and sharp, and underneath that the chalk, and underneath that, nothing. The fever doesn't allow for tasting. The fever only allows for making.
The first piece is called THE CONVERSATION.

It starts with a 1620s doublet — I know the silhouette the way I know my own skeleton, because it was my skeleton once. The stiff collar, the padded shoulders, the narrow waist that men of property wore like armor. I draft the pattern from memory and my hands move with a precision that shouldn't exist in fingers this tired, this tremor-prone. But they don't tremble. Not during this. Not while I'm cutting.

The wool falls away from the shears in a clean line. The sound — that sound, silk under scissors, wool parting — it's the same sound it was four hundred years ago. Fabric doesn't change its nature for the century. Only the shape changes.

I cut the collar and my hands remember before my mind does. This collar. I've made this collar before. 1623. A funeral doublet — black broadcloth, silver buttons, sewn for the burial of a man whose name I've deliberately forgotten. Different fabric. Different purpose. Same hands. My fingers know the curve of the neck, the depth of the neckline, the exact angle where the collar stands proud of the shoulder. They knew it before I was Orlando. Before I was anyone with a name at all.
The hum pulses through my wrist and into the chalk like a second pulse.

I don't fight it. I overlay the doublet with a 2026 bomber jacket — the same structured shoulders, but slouched now, broken-in, streetwise. The ancestral silhouette of the male body I once wore, fused with the streetwear of the body I wear now. Two timelines in one garment. A conversation across centuries, stitched into the seams.

The zipper track runs where buttonholes used to be. The cuffs are ribbed knit instead of stiffened linen. But the shoulders — the shoulders are exactly the same. Four hundred years, and the shoulders haven't changed. We still want to look broader than we are. We still want to take up more space than we're allowed.

I pin the pieces together and stand back and look at it on the dress form and the hum says yes, yes, yes — not in words, in frequency, in the way it brightens and steadies when I've gotten something right.
THE CONVERSATION. Century one. Done.
I reach for the next bolt of fabric and I don't stop.
Day five.
I know it's day five because Sable calls, and Sable always calls on day five. She has a clock in her brain calibrated to my self-destruction.
"Hello?" I say, and my voice sounds like gravel dragged across sandpaper.
"You sound like you're dying."
"I'm creating." I pin a dart with one hand, press the phone to my ear with the other. "It feels similar."
A pause. When Sable pauses, it means she's choosing between several true things she could say. She always picks the one that hurts most precisely.
"The last time you worked like this, you didn't leave your studio for three months. That was in 1847."
My hands stop. The pin hovers over the fabric. The hum stutters, flickers, steadies.
"You remember that?"
"I remember everything you've told me." Her voice is warm and afraid at the same time — that specific Sable frequency, caretaker and witness. "Be careful. The fever takes as much as it gives."
"I know."
"Do you? Because you didn't eat in 1847 either, and you looked like a ghost for a year afterward."
"I'm eating."
"Says the woman whose assistant has sent her fourteen texts about food delivery."
"Suki's dramatic."
"Suki's accurate. Call me tomorrow."

She hangs up. I stare at the phone. The screen shows six unread messages from Suki, one from an unknown number — a journalist, deleted without reading — and a calendar reminder: SHOWCASE IN NINE DAYS.
Nine days.
I put the phone down and pick up the shears. The hum resumes its purr. The chalk dust swirls.
Nine days is a lifetime. Nine days is nothing. Both things are true, and neither of them matters, because the next piece is already forming in my hands.
Day ten. The VENICE piece.

I unroll the midnight blue silk across the cutting table and the color hits me like a body blow. This blue. I know this blue. I wore this blue in 1743 — not wore, inhabited, lived inside it for the worst year of a century I've mostly tried to forget. The mourning dress. Marco's dress. The one I sewed while his body was still warm on the memory, the one I finished after the funeral because finishing it was the only thing left to do with my hands.
But this is not that dress.

I pin the boat collar first — the same wide, elegant collar that framed my face when my face was framed for grief. The same sleeves, generous and draping, meant to hide how thin my arms had gotten. But the hem — the hem I don't finish clean. I cut it raw. Irregular. Deliberately uneven, like a shoreline, like something the water has touched and retreated from, like something that refuses to be smooth or finished or done.
Marco's dress, but finished with rage instead of grief.

The silk whispers under my shears. That sound — there's no sound like it, the parting of silk under a sharp blade, the clean surrender of it — and I'm cutting and pinning and the hum surges through my wrists and into the fabric and it's not a warning, not a threat, not the pre-shift vibration that means my body is about to betray me.
It's a YES.
The hum is saying: this is right. This is what you were made to do.

My hands move faster. I pin the bodice, I shape the waist, I ease the fabric over the dress form's hips and the blue catches the overhead light and glows like the Grand Canal at midnight and I can see Marco in it — not his face, I can never see his face anymore, but the shape of him, the way grief has a silhouette, the way loss can be cut and sewn and worn.

But this dress doesn't wear grief. This dress wears what comes after grief. The part where you stop kneeling and start standing. The part where you take the thing that broke you and you stitch it into something that makes you terrifying.

I step back. The dress form wears the VENICE piece like a challenge. Midnight blue silk, raw hem, boat collar open like a wound that chose to stay open. On the back, I've sewn a single line of invisible stitching — a seam that only exists if you know to look for it. Marco's initials, hidden in the construction. A secret. A burial. A consecration.
The hum is a steady, bright drone. Not building toward anything. Not threatening. Just present. Just approving.
I've never felt the hum approve of anything before. I don't know if that's better or worse.
Day fourteen.
The studio is a war zone and a cathedral.

Dress forms stand at attention around the perimeter like soldiers — twelve of them, each wearing a different century. Fabric drapes from every surface: pinned to the walls, slung over chairs, pooled on the floor in rivers of color and texture. Chalk outlines cover the concrete like crime scene diagrams. My shears are dull — I should have replaced them days ago, but there's no time, so I press harder, and the fabric still parts because four hundred years of pressure teaches you how to compensate for a dull edge.
THE CONVERSATION. Century one. 1620s doublet meets 2026 bomber. The shoulders are perfect. The shoulders are always perfect.

THE MASK. Century two. 1700s Venice, but not the Venice of tourists — the Venice of flood tides and theater. A bauta hood reimagined as a high-collared cape, the kind of garment that conceals and reveals in the same instant. Black wool and gold thread. Anonymity as couture.

THE MOURNING. Century three. 1800s. William's century. A Victorian crinoline silhouette stripped to its bones — the cage visible, the structure exposed, the petticoat replaced with sheer organza that hides nothing. I made this one crying. I'm not ashamed of that. The hum didn't flinch when I cried. It just kept vibrating through my fingers while I measured and cut and wept and pinned.

THE UTILITY. Century four. 1900s. Wartime. A utility jumpsuit — the kind women wore in factories while the men were dying overseas — but tailored to within an inch of its life. Broad shoulders, nipped waist, patch pockets that are actually functional because function is the point. When the world is on fire, beauty is not useless. Beauty is how you remember the world is worth saving.

And eight more. Eight more pieces that bridge past and present in ways that shouldn't work but do, because my hands have four hundred years of muscle memory and they know how to make a seventeenth-century seam meet a twenty-first-century stitch line without either one complaining. There's the REBEL (1970s denim meets 1670s embroidery), the SIGNAL (1920s beading on a 1620s bodice), the ECHO (a coat that references every century at once — layered, palimpsestic, a garment you could read like a timeline if you knew the language).

And I'm not done. I'm not close to done. The showcase is days away and I'm standing in the middle of the studio with pins between my lips — the taste of metal bright and constant on my tongue — and chalk on my face, probably, I haven't looked in a mirror in days, and fabric between my fingers, midnight blue, always coming back to midnight blue. The hum is a constant low vibration now. Not building toward a shift. Not warning me. Just present. Like a second heartbeat. Like something that has always been inside me but never been on my side before.
My phone buzzes. Suki: EAT. NOW. I'm not asking.
I don't eat. I reach for the next piece of fabric — a deep burgundy wool, heavy and rich, the kind of fabric that weighs as much as a promise — and I think:
This is what I've been saving myself for.

Not survival. Not hiding. Not the endless calculus of when to shift and what to wear and how to be invisible in yet another century. This. Making something that could only be made by someone who lived through all of it. Every funeral. Every love. Every loss. Every body. Wearing it all at once, in twelve garments, on a runway, in the city where I chose to stop running.
The hum pulses. I pin the wool.

The showcase is coming. The hum is getting louder. And something is building — not a shift, not a threat, but something. A frequency I've never heard before, buried in the vibration. As if the hum, after four hundred years of sabotage, is trying to tell me something.
I just don't know if it's a warning or a promise.
I reach for the shears.
